lines:
The three companions had been walking since dawn, following a map that grew less trustworthy with every mile. The knight insisted they were close. The wizard insisted the knight had said that three hours ago. The thief, wisely, said nothing and kept walking.

By the time the sun dipped behind the ridge, they'd found exactly one thing of note: a signpost, half-buried in brambles, pointing in a direction none of them had come from. They took it anyway. It seemed rude not to.

Long before the dungeon had monsters in it, it had simply been a very ambitious wine cellar, built by a merchant who badly overestimated how much wine one household could need. When the merchant's fortune collapsed, so did his plans, and the cellar was left half-finished, sprawling, and forgotten.

It was the slimes who moved in first, drawn by the damp stone and the leftover barrels. The goblins came later, evicted from a nearby hill by something considerably larger than themselves. Nobody remembers exactly when it became a proper dungeon. It simply happened, the way these things tend to.

The Slime King did not, in fact, start out as a king of anything. He was, by most accounts, an unusually large slime who happened to be sitting in the right cave when a traveling bard needed a villain for her next song.

The name stuck. So did the crown, which he found half-buried near the cave entrance and has refused to remove ever since, mostly because nobody has worked out how slimes are meant to wear crowns in the first place. He seems to like it regardless.

Every adventurer eventually learns the difference between a real treasure chest and a mimic, though the lesson is rarely a gentle one. The old trick is to toss a coin at it first; a real chest stays put, while a mimic, offended at being tested, usually can't help but twitch.

Of course, a sufficiently patient mimic has learned to hold still anyway, which is exactly the kind of thing that keeps this lesson interesting generation after generation. Most adventurers still throw the coin. Most of them still get it wrong sometimes.
